sentence: I enjoy ______ to music while I work.
explanation: <Enjoy> is always followed by the gerund (-ing form).

sentence: She decided ______ a new language.
explanation: <Decide> is always followed by the infinitive (to + verb).

sentence: Do you mind ______ the window?
explanation: <Mind> is always followed by the gerund (-ing form).

sentence: They plan ______ to Spain next summer.
explanation: <Plan> is always followed by the infinitive (to + verb).

sentence: He avoids ______ fast food.
explanation: <Avoid> is always followed by the gerund (-ing form).

sentence: We hope ______ you at the party.
explanation: <Hope> is always followed by the infinitive (to + verb).

sentence: She suggested ______ to the new restaurant.
explanation: <Suggest> is always followed by the gerund (-ing form).

sentence: He refused ______ the contract.
explanation: <Refuse> is always followed by the infinitive (to + verb).

sentence: I finished ______ the report at midnight.
explanation: <Finish> is always followed by the gerund (-ing form).

sentence: She promised ______ me back.
explanation: <Promise> is always followed by the infinitive (to + verb).

sentence: He keeps ______ the same mistakes.
explanation: <Keep> is always followed by the gerund (-ing form).

sentence: I can't afford ______ a new car right now.
explanation: <Afford> is always followed by the infinitive (to + verb).

sentence: She considered ______ her job.
explanation: <Consider> is always followed by the gerund (-ing form).

sentence: He agreed ______ us with the project.
explanation: <Agree> is always followed by the infinitive (to + verb).

sentence: I miss ______ my old school friends.
explanation: <Miss> is always followed by the gerund (-ing form).

sentence: They offered ______ us to the airport.
explanation: <Offer> is always followed by the infinitive (to + verb).

sentence: He denied ______ the money.
explanation: <Deny> is always followed by the gerund (-ing form).

sentence: She expects ______ a promotion this year.
explanation: <Expect> is always followed by the infinitive (to + verb).

sentence: I'm interested in ______ more about history.
explanation: After prepositions like <interested in>, always use the gerund.

sentence: She managed ______ the exam on her first try.
explanation: <Manage> is always followed by the infinitive (to + verb).

sentence: He gave up ______ two years ago.
explanation: <Give up> is always followed by the gerund (-ing form).

sentence: I look forward to ______ you next week.
explanation: In <look forward to>, the word 'to' is a preposition, so use the gerund.

sentence: She pretended ______ asleep when I came in.
explanation: <Pretend> is always followed by the infinitive (to + verb).

sentence: He risked ______ his job by speaking up.
explanation: <Risk> is always followed by the gerund (-ing form).

sentence: We need ______ more before the presentation.
explanation: <Need> is always followed by the infinitive (to + verb).
